title: Regular Expression Matching (., *)
description: Implement regex matching with . and *. Return YES/NO. Example: s=aa, p=a* -> YES

examples.explanation: Step 1: i=0 (s index), j=0 (p index). p[1]='*', so we have two branches: Branch A (skip): match(0,2) → i=0, j=2. Branch B (match one): match(1,0) → i=1, j=0. Explore Branch A: i=0, j=2. j=2 is end of pattern, i=0 not end of string → return NO. Backtrack to Branch B: i=1, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B1: match(1,2) → i=1, j=2. Branch B2: match(2,0) → i=2, j=0. Explore Branch B1: i=1, j=2. j=2 is end, i=1 not end → NO. Backtrack to Branch B2: i=2, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B2a: match(2,2) → i=2, j=2. Branch B2b: match(3,0) → i=3, j=0 (out of bounds). Branch B2a: i=2, j=2, both ends → YES. Return YES.

Step 1: i=0, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch A: match(0,2) → i=0, j=2. Branch B: match(1,0) → i=1, j=0. Branch A: i=0, j=2. j=2 is end, i=0 not end → NO. Branch B: i=1, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B1: match(1,2) → i=1, j=2. Branch B2: match(2,0) → i=2, j=0. Branch B1: i=1, j=2. j=2 end, i=1 not end → NO. Branch B2: i=2, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B2a: match(2,2) → i=2, j=2. Branch B2b: match(3,0) → out of bounds. Branch B2a: i=2, j=2, both ends → YES. Return YES.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(n+m))
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible matches by handling . as any single character and * as zero or more repetitions of the preceding character. This approach explores all combinations without optimization, making it straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Regular Expression Matching with Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of regex matching with . and * using exhaustive recursion.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the regular expression matching problem using brute force recursion. The intuition is to explore all possible ways the pattern could match the string by recursively handling two special characters: '.' matches any single character, and '*' means zero or more repetitions of the preceding character. We start comparing from the beginning of both string and pattern. At each step, we check if the current pattern character has a '*' following it. If it does, we have two possibilities: either skip the current pattern character and its '*' (match zero occurrences), or match one character from the string and keep the '*' for potential further matches. If there's no '*', we simply match the current characters (with '.' matching anything). The recursion continues until we reach the end of both string and pattern, or we find a mismatch. The time complexity is O(2^(n+m)) because in the worst case (with many '*' patterns), each position can branch into two recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the recursion stack. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs because it explores all combinations without memoization or optimization. Common mistakes include not properly handling the zero-match case for '*', forgetting to check pattern bounds before accessing characters, and not handling the case where string is empty but pattern has '*' patterns that can match zero characters. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete recursive matching logic described in the key subroutine.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0 (s index), j=0 (p index). p[1]='*', so we have two branches: Branch A (skip): match(0,2) → i=0, j=2. Branch B (match one): match(1,0) → i=1, j=0. Explore Branch A: i=0, j=2. j=2 is end of pattern, i=0 not end of string → return NO. Backtrack to Branch B: i=1, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B1: match(1,2) → i=1, j=2. Branch B2: match(2,0) → i=2, j=0. Explore Branch B1: i=1, j=2. j=2 is end, i=1 not end → NO. Backtrack to Branch B2: i=2, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B2a: match(2,2) → i=2, j=2. Branch B2b: match(3,0) → i=3, j=0 (out of bounds). Branch B2a: i=2, j=2, both ends → YES. Return YES.

Step 1: i=0, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch A: match(0,2) → i=0, j=2. Branch B: match(1,0) → i=1, j=0. Branch A: i=0, j=2. j=2 is end, i=0 not end → NO. Branch B: i=1, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B1: match(1,2) → i=1, j=2. Branch B2: match(2,0) → i=2, j=0. Branch B1: i=1, j=2. j=2 end, i=1 not end → NO. Branch B2: i=2, j=0. p[1]='*', two branches: Branch B2a: match(2,2) → i=2, j=2. Branch B2b: match(3,0) → out of bounds. Branch B2a: i=2, j=2, both ends → YES. Return YES.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Matching Function with Star Handling
ladders.desc: The core recursive function that matches string against pattern at given indices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function match(i, j) that returns whether string s[i:] matches pattern p[j:]. The intuition is to break down the problem into smaller subproblems by advancing indices. We first check if we've reached the end of both string and pattern (base case: success). If pattern is exhausted but string isn't, it's a failure. The key insight is handling '*': when p[j+1] is '*', we have two recursive possibilities: 1) Skip the current pattern character and its '*' (match zero occurrences): call match(i, j+2). 2) Match one character if current string character matches pattern character (or pattern is '.'), then recursively match remaining string with same pattern (keeping '*' for potential further matches): call match(i+1, j). If there's no '*', we simply match current characters and advance both indices. Time complexity for this function in isolation is O(2^(n+m)) in worst case due to branching. Space is O(n+m) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 as the complete solution repeatedly calls this function. Common mistakes: not checking j+1 < len(p) before accessing p[j+1] for '*', incorrect base case order, and infinite recursion when matching '*' without advancing string index in zero-match case. The pseudocode pattern: def match(i, j): if j == len(p): return i == len(s); first_match = i < len(s) and (p[j] == s[i] or p[j] == '.'); if j+1 < len(p) and p[j+1] == '*': return match(i, j+2) or (first_match and match(i+1, j)); else: return first_match and match(i+1, j+1).

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0. j=0 not end. first_match = (0<1 and ('a'=='a' or 'a'=='.')) = True. p[1]='*', so two branches: Branch A: match(0,2) → i=0, j=2. j=2 is end, i=0 not end → return False. Branch B: first_match and match(1,0) → True and match(1,0). match(1,0): i=1, j=0. first_match = (1<1 and ...) = False. p[1]='*', so match(1,2) → i=1, j=2. j=2 end, i=1 end → return True. So Branch B returns True. Overall return True (YES).

Step 1: i=0, j=0. first_match = (0<1 and (p[0]=a==s[0]=b or p[0]=a==.)) = False. p[1]=*, so two branches: Branch A (skip): call match(0,2) -> i=0, j=2. j=2 is end of pattern, i=0 not end of string -> return False. Branch B (use): first_match and match(1,0) -> False and ... -> False. Return Branch A OR Branch B = False OR False = False (NO).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Star (*) Operator with Recursive Branching
ladders.desc: The central technique of exploring zero or more matches using two recursive branches.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic for handling the '*' operator in the pattern. The intuition is that '*' represents zero or more repetitions of the preceding character, so when we encounter a pattern like 'a*', we need to consider all possible numbers of 'a's that could match the string. This is achieved by branching the recursion into two cases: 1) Zero-match branch: skip the 'a*' entirely by moving the pattern index past the '*' (j+2). This corresponds to matching zero occurrences of 'a'. 2) One-or-more-match branch: if the current string character matches the pattern character (or pattern is '.'), match one character by advancing the string index (i+1) but keep the pattern index at the same position (j) to allow for more matches. This branching explores all possibilities from zero to many matches. The time complexity for this logic alone is exponential in worst case because each '*' can double the number of recursive calls. Space is linear due to recursion depth. This feeds into L1 as it's the key part of the matching function. Common mistakes: forgetting the zero-match branch, not checking string bounds before matching one character, and incorrectly advancing pattern index in the one-or-more branch. The pseudocode: if p[j+1] == '*': return match(i, j+2) or (first_match and match(i+1, j)). Real pattern: This is a classic recursive backtracking pattern where we explore both inclusion and exclusion cases.

examples.input: Handle '*' for s='aa', p='a*' at i=0, j=0
examples.trace: At i=0, j=0: p[0]='a', p[1]='*'. first_match = (0<2 and ('a'=='a' or 'a'=='.')) = True. Two branches: Branch 1 (zero-match): match(0,2) → skip 'a*', pattern becomes empty, string 'aa' remains → will fail unless string empty. Branch 2 (one-or-more): first_match is True, so match(1,0) → match one 'a', string becomes 'a', pattern stays 'a*'. Then recursively: at i=1, j=0: first_match = True, same branching: zero-match: match(1,2) → pattern empty, string 'a' → fail. one-or-more: match(2,0) → string empty, pattern 'a*'. Then at i=2, j=0: first_match = (2<2 and ...) = False, zero-match: match(2,2) → both empty → success. So overall success via one-or-more branch.

examples.input: Handle '*' for s='b', p='a*' at i=0, j=0
examples.trace: At i=0, j=0: p[0]='a', p[1]='*'. first_match = (0<1 and ('a'=='b' or 'a'=='.')) = False. Two branches: Branch 1 (zero-match): match(0,2) → skip 'a*', pattern empty, string 'b' → fail because string not empty. Branch 2 (one-or-more): first_match and match(1,0) → False and ... → False. So overall fail. This shows how zero-match branch is tried even when first_match is False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matching Single Characters with Dot (.) Operator
ladders.desc: The intermediate concept of matching individual characters where '.' matches any character.
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the basic character matching without '*'. The intuition is simple: at each step, we need to check if the current pattern character matches the current string character. A regular character like 'a' matches only 'a', while the special character '.' matches any single character (e.g., 'a', 'b', etc.). This is a fundamental operation that feeds into the star handling logic, as before deciding what to do with '*', we first check if the current characters match. The algorithm: given indices i and j, if i is within string bounds and j is within pattern bounds, check if p[j] == s[i] or p[j] == '.'. If true, characters match; if false, they don't match. This check is used in both cases: when there's no '*', we advance both indices if match succeeds; when there's '*', we use this to determine if the one-or-more branch is possible. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 as it's part of the first_match calculation. Common mistakes: not checking string bounds before accessing s[i], forgetting that '.' matches any character including digits or symbols, and confusing '.' with '*'. The pseudocode: first_match = i < len(s) and (p[j] == s[i] or p[j] == '.'). Real pattern: This is a standard character comparison with a wildcard condition.

examples.input: Match single char: s='cat', p='c.t' at i=1, j=1
examples.trace: i=1 → s[1]='a'. j=1 → p[1]='.'. Check: i<3? Yes. p[j]=='.'? Yes. So first_match = True. This allows the match to proceed because '.' matches 'a'.

examples.input: Match single char: s='dog', p='d.g' at i=1, j=1
examples.trace: i=1 → s[1]='o'. j=1 → p[1]='.'. Check: i<3? Yes. p[j]=='.'? Yes. first_match = True. '.' matches 'o'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Index Manipulation and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of moving indices and ensuring they stay within string/pattern bounds.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations of managing indices i (for string s) and j (for pattern p) during recursion. The intuition is that we need to track our position in both the string and pattern as we attempt to match them. Key operations: 1) Increment i to move to next string character when a match occurs. 2) Increment j to move to next pattern character when no '*' is involved or when skipping a '*'. 3) Check bounds: before accessing s[i] or p[j], ensure i < len(s) and j < len(p) to avoid index errors. 4) Base cases: when j reaches len(p), check if i also reaches len(s) for full match. These operations are crucial for the recursion to terminate correctly and avoid runtime errors. Time complexity per operation is O(1). Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as bounds checking is needed for character matching. Common mistakes: forgetting to check bounds leading to IndexError, incorrectly updating indices (e.g., advancing j by 1 instead of 2 for '*'), and not handling empty strings or patterns properly. The pseudocode involves simple if statements and arithmetic: if j == len(p): return i == len(s); if i < len(s): ...; if j+1 < len(p): .... Real pattern: This is standard index management in recursive string processing.

examples.input: Indices for s='ab', p='a' starting at i=0, j=0
examples.trace: Start: i=0, j=0. Match s[0]='a' and p[0]='a' → success. Increment i to 1, j to 1. Now j=1 equals len(p)=1 → base case: check i=1 vs len(s)=2 → i not equal, so return False. Indices show we matched 'a' but string has leftover 'b'.

examples.input: Indices for s='', p='a*' starting at i=0, j=0
examples.trace: Start: i=0, j=0. p[1]='*', so we skip: set j to j+2=2. Now j=2 equals len(p)=2 → base case: i=0 equals len(s)=0 → return True. Indices show we skipped pattern entirely.

testCases.expected: YES (i end, j end after skip)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: recursive function calls and termination conditions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces recursion, a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. The intuition is to break a complex problem into similar but smaller subproblems. For regex matching, we recursively match prefixes of the string and pattern. Key concepts: 1) Recursive case: the function calls itself with modified arguments (e.g., advanced indices). 2) Base case: conditions where recursion stops to prevent infinite loops. Here, base cases are: when pattern index reaches end (check if string index also at end), or when mismatch occurs. 3) Problem reduction: each recursive call works on a smaller problem (shorter string or pattern). Time and space complexity depend on recursion depth; here, depth up to n+m. This feeds into L4 as recursion relies on index manipulation. Common mistakes: missing base cases causing infinite recursion, not reducing problem size in recursive calls, and stack overflow for deep recursion. The pseudocode: def solve(subproblem): if base_case: return result; else: return combine(solve(smaller_subproblem)). Real pattern: This is fundamental to many DSA problems like tree traversal or divide-and-conquer.

examples.trace: Function count(n): if n<0: return; else: print(n); count(n-1). Call count(3): print 3; call count(2): print 2; call count(1): print 1; call count(0): print 0; call count(-1): base case, return. Recursion stops.

Function fact(n): if n==0: return 1; else: return n * fact(n-1). Call fact(4): return 4 * fact(3). fact(3): return 3 * fact(2). fact(2): return 2 * fact(1). fact(1): return 1 * fact(0). fact(0): return 1. Unwind: 1*1=1, 2*1=2, 3*2=6, 4*6=24.

testCases.input: Recursive binary search on sorted array
Recursive tower of Hanoi with 3 disks

approaches.name: Approach 2: Top-Down DP with Memoization O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems (i, j) where i is index in string and j in pattern, avoiding redundant calculations. This improves efficiency by caching outcomes, making it suitable for medium-sized inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Regular Expression Matching with Top-Down DP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of regex matching with . and * using memoized recursion
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the full regular expression matching problem using top-down dynamic programming with memoization. The intuition is to break the problem into overlapping subproblems: for each position (i,j) in the string and pattern, we determine if the remaining portions match. We use recursion to explore all possibilities and memoization to avoid redundant calculations. The key insight is handling the * operator correctly: when we encounter p[j+1] = '*', we have two choices: skip the current pattern character (use it 0 times) or use it at least once if it matches the current string character. For '.' it matches any single character. The algorithm starts from (0,0) and recursively explores all valid paths. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is string length and m is pattern length, as each state (i,j) is computed once. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the memoization table plus recursion stack. This approach is efficient for medium-sized inputs and naturally handles the complex branching logic of regex matching. Common mistakes include: not handling empty string with pattern correctly, forgetting to check bounds before accessing characters, and incorrect handling of * operator cases. The solution connects to L1 by using the recursive matching function as the core subroutine.

examples.trace:
1. Start: i=0, j=0, s='aa', p='a*'
2. Check memo[0][0] - not computed
3. p[1] = '*', so handle star case
4. Option 1: Skip 'a*' (use 0 times): call match(0,2)
   - i=0, j=2: j==m (2), i==n (0)? No, i=0 < n=2
   - Return NO
5. Option 2: Use 'a' at least once: first_char_match = (i < n) and (p[0] == '.' or p[0] == s[0]) = True
6. Call match(1,0): i=1, j=0
7. p[1] = '*', handle star case again
8. Option 1: Skip 'a*': call match(1,2)
   - i=1, j=2: j==m (2), i==n (2)? No, i=1 < n=2
   - Return NO
9. Option 2: Use 'a' at least once: first_char_match = (1 < 2) and ('a' == '.' or 'a' == 'a') = True
10. Call match(2,0): i=2, j=0
11. p[1] = '*', handle star case
12. Option 1: Skip 'a*': call match(2,2)
    - i=2, j=2: j==m (2), i==n (2)? Yes, both at end
    - Return YES
13. memo[2][0] = YES
14. Backtrack: memo[1][0] = YES (from option 2)
15. Backtrack: memo[0][0] = YES (from option 2)
16. Final result: YES

1. Start: i=0, j=0, s='ab', p='.*'
2. Check memo[0][0] - not computed
3. p[1] = '*', so handle star case
4. Option 1: Skip '.*' (use 0 times): call match(0,2)
   - i=0, j=2: j==m (2), i==n (2)? No, i=0 < n=2
   - Return NO
5. Option 2: Use '.' at least once: first_char_match = (i < n) and (p[0] == '.' or p[0] == s[0]) = (0<2) and ('.' == '.' or '.' == 'a') = True
6. Call match(1,0): i=1, j=0
7. p[1] = '*', handle star case again
8. Option 1: Skip '.*': call match(1,2)
   - i=1, j=2: j==m (2), i==n (2)? No, i=1 < n=2
   - Return NO
9. Option 2: Use '.' at least once: first_char_match = (1<2) and ('.' == '.' or '.' == 'b') = True
10. Call match(2,0): i=2, j=0
11. p[1] = '*', handle star case
12. Option 1: Skip '.*': call match(2,2)
    - i=2, j=2: j==m (2), i==n (2)? Yes
    - Return YES
13. memo[2][0] = YES
14. Backtrack: memo[1][0] = YES
15. Backtrack: memo[0][0] = YES
16. Final result: YES

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Matching Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: The core recursive function that determines if s[i:] matches p[j:]
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the top-down DP solution - a recursive function match(i,j) that returns whether the substring s[i:] matches the pattern substring p[j:]. The intuition is that regex matching has optimal substructure: the match for the entire strings depends on matches of their suffixes. We define match(i,j) = True if s[i:] matches p[j:], False otherwise. The base cases are: 1) if j reaches pattern end (j == m), return whether i reached string end (i == n). 2) Otherwise, check if there's a star at next position (j+1 < m and p[j+1] == '*'). If yes, we have two options: skip the current pattern character (match(i, j+2)) or use it at least once if current characters match (first_match and match(i+1, j)). If no star, we simply check if current characters match and proceed to next positions (first_match and match(i+1, j+1)). We memoize results in a 2D array dp[i][j] to avoid recomputation. Time complexity for this function called on (0,0) is O(n*m) as each state is computed once. Space is O(n*m) for memo table. This connects to L0 as it's the complete solution function. Common mistakes: forgetting to check i < n before accessing s[i], incorrect base case logic, not handling the star operator's two cases properly.

examples.trace:
1. Call match(0,0)
2. j=0 < m=1, i=0 < n=1
3. Check star: j+1=1 == m=1, so no star
4. first_match = (i < n) and (p[0] == '.' or p[0] == s[0]) = True and ('a' == '.' or 'a' == 'a') = True
5. Return first_match and match(1,1)
6. Call match(1,1)
7. j=1 == m=1, i=1 == n=1, return True
8. Backtrack: match(0,0) = True and True = True
9. Result: YES

1. Call match(0,0)
2. j=0 < m=2, i=0 < n=0
3. Check star: j+1=1 < m=2 and p[1]='*', so star exists
4. Option 1: Skip 'a*': call match(0,2)
   - j=2 == m=2, i=0 == n=0, return True
5. Option 2: Use 'a' at least once: first_match = (i < n) and (p[0] == '.' or p[0] == s[?]) = False and ... = False
6. Return Option 1 or Option 2 = True or False = True
7. Result: YES

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling the Star Operator (*)
ladders.desc: The branching logic for pattern characters followed by *
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the most complex part of regex matching: handling the * operator which means 'zero or more of the preceding element'. When we encounter a pattern character at position j followed by * at j+1, we have two mutually exclusive possibilities that we must explore: 1) Use the preceding character ZERO times - this means we skip both the character and the *, moving to j+2 in the pattern while keeping the same string position i. 2) Use the preceding character AT LEAST ONCE - this requires that the current string character matches the pattern character (or pattern character is '.'), and then we recursively check with i+1 (consuming one string character) but keeping j at the same position (since * allows multiple repetitions). The intuition is that * creates a branching point in our search tree. We need to explore both branches because either could lead to a match. This is where the exponential complexity would come without memoization. The key insight is that these two options cover all possibilities: if we choose option 2 (use at least once), we can subsequently choose option 2 again to use more times, or option 1 to stop using it. Time complexity for this logic alone, if implemented naively, could be exponential, but with memoization it becomes O(n*m). This connects to L1 as it's a critical part of the recursive function. Common mistakes: treating the two options as sequential rather than branching (OR relationship), forgetting to check if current characters match before using option 2, and not considering that option 1 (skip) is always available regardless of character match.

examples.trace:
At position (0,0):
Pattern has 'a' at j=0, '*' at j=1
Branch 1 (Skip):
- Don't use 'a', move to j=2
- Check if s[0:] matches p[2:]
- p[2:] is empty, s[0:] is 'aa'
- Result: NO
Branch 2 (Use at least once):
- Check if s[0] matches 'a': YES
- Use one 'a', move to i=1, keep j=0
- Now at (1,0) with same pattern 'a*'
- Repeat branching...
- Eventually reaches YES when i=2, j=2
Final result: YES (from branch 2)

At position (0,0):
Pattern has 'a' at j=0, '*' at j=1
Branch 1 (Skip):
- Skip 'a*', move to j=2
- Now check if s[0:]='b' matches p[2:]='b'
- Characters match: 'b' == 'b'
- Move to (1,3): both ends reached
- Result: YES
Branch 2 (Use at least once):
- Check if s[0] matches 'a': NO ('b' != 'a')
- This branch fails immediately
Final result: YES (from branch 1)

ladders.title: Building Block — Character Matching with Dot Operator
ladders.desc: Determining if a single string character matches a pattern character
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks if a single character from the string matches a single character from the pattern, accounting for the special '.' operator. The intuition is simple: a pattern character matches a string character if either 1) the pattern character is exactly equal to the string character, or 2) the pattern character is '.' which matches any single character. However, we must also handle edge cases: we can only attempt matching if we haven't exhausted the string (i < n). If the string is exhausted but pattern isn't, matching fails unless the pattern has a * operator (handled at higher level). This operation is performed before advancing to next positions in both string and pattern. In pseudocode: first_match = (i < n) and (p[j] == '.' or p[j] == s[i]). This is a constant-time operation. It connects to L2 as it's needed for the 'use at least once' branch of star handling and for normal character advancement. Common mistakes: forgetting to check i < n before accessing s[i], not considering that '.' matches any character including empty string (it doesn't - '.' matches exactly one character), and confusing this with the * operator logic.

examples.trace:
1. Check i < n: 0 < 1 = True
2. Check p[0] == '.' or p[0] == s[0]: 'a' == '.' or 'a' == 'a'
3. 'a' == '.' is False
4. 'a' == 'a' is True
5. False or True = True
6. Result: True

1. Check i < n: 0 < 1 = True
2. Check p[0] == '.' or p[0] == s[0]: '.' == '.' or '.' == 'b'
3. '.' == '.' is True
4. True or anything = True
5. Result: True

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String and Pattern Index Manipulation
ladders.desc: Moving through indices i (string) and j (pattern) with bounds checking
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest operational component: correctly incrementing indices i and j while staying within bounds. The intuition is that we're processing the string and pattern from left to right, so we need to track our current position in each. Key operations: 1) Check if we've reached the end of string (i == n) or pattern (j == m). 2) Safely access characters s[i] and p[j] only when i < n and j < m. 3) Increment i to move to next string character. 4) Increment j to move to next pattern character. 5) Special case: when skipping a pattern character followed by *, we increment j by 2. This is constant-time work. It connects to L3 as index manipulation is needed for character matching. Common mistakes: accessing array out of bounds (e.g., s[i] when i == n), forgetting that string and pattern may have different lengths, and incorrect increment amounts (especially with * operator).

examples.trace:
Start: i=0, j=0
1. Access s[0]='a', p[0]='a' (both in bounds)
2. Characters match, so increment: i=1, j=1
3. Access s[1]='b', p[1]='b' (both in bounds)
4. Characters match, so increment: i=2, j=2
5. Now j=2 == m=2 (pattern end), i=2 < n=3 (string not end)
6. Result: mismatch because pattern exhausted but string not

Start: i=0, j=0
1. Detect p[1]='*' (j+1=1 < m=2)
2. Option: skip 'a*' by setting j=2 directly
3. Now j=2 == m=2, i=0 < n=1
4. Pattern exhausted but string not: mismatch
5. Alternative: use 'a' once: i=1, j=0 (j stays same for *)
6. Now i=1 == n=1, j=0 < m=2
7. Continue processing...

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Basic recursive thinking with simple string/pattern termination conditions
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces recursion in the context of string matching: a function that calls itself with smaller inputs. The intuition is that matching entire strings can be reduced to matching their prefixes/suffixes. We need clear base cases - conditions where recursion stops: 1) If pattern is empty (j == m), return whether string is also empty (i == n). 2) If string is empty but pattern isn't, we can't return False immediately because pattern might contain * operators that match zero characters (handled at higher levels). For this basic level, we consider only exact character matching without special operators. Example: match('abc', 'abc') reduces to match('bc', 'bc') reduces to match('c', 'c') reduces to match('', '') which returns True. Time complexity is O(min(n,m)) in this simple case. Space complexity is O(min(n,m)) for recursion depth. This connects to L4 as recursion uses index manipulation. Common mistakes: infinite recursion (no base case), incorrect base case logic, not reducing problem size in recursive calls.

examples.trace:
match('abc', 'abc'):
1. Both non-empty
2. s[0]='a' matches p[0]='a'
3. Recursive call: match('bc', 'bc')
4. s[0]='b' matches p[0]='b'
5. Recursive call: match('c', 'c')
6. s[0]='c' matches p[0]='c'
7. Recursive call: match('', '')
8. Base case: both empty, return True
9. Backtrack: all return True
10. Final: YES

match('ab', 'abc'):
1. Both non-empty
2. s[0]='a' matches p[0]='a'
3. Recursive call: match('b', 'bc')
4. s[0]='b' matches p[0]='b'
5. Recursive call: match('', 'c')
6. Base case: string empty, pattern not empty
7. Return False
8. Backtrack: False propagates
9. Final: NO

approaches.name: Approach 3: Bottom-Up DP Tabulation O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Build a DP table iteratively from the base cases up to the full string and pattern lengths, filling entries based on character matches and * handling. This avoids recursion overhead and is often more space-efficient with optimizations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bottom-Up DP for Regular Expression Matching
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of regex matching with . and * using bottom-up DP tabulation.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the regular expression matching problem using bottom-up dynamic programming. The intuition is to build a DP table where dp[i][j] represents whether the first i characters of string s match the first j characters of pattern p. We fill this table iteratively from the base cases up to the full lengths, avoiding recursion overhead. The key insight is that we can handle * by considering two possibilities: either the character before * matches zero times (skip the pattern), or it matches one or more times (consume a character from the string). The . character matches any single character. We start with dp[0][0] = True (empty string matches empty pattern), then fill the first row for patterns that can match empty strings (like a*, a*b*, etc.). For each cell (i,j), if p[j-1] is not '*', we check if characters match and look at dp[i-1][j-1]. If p[j-1] is '*', we check either dp[i][j-2] (skip the pattern) or (match current character and look at dp[i-1][j]). The final answer is dp[n][m]. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is string length and m is pattern length. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the DP table, though it can be optimized to O(m). This approach is efficient and avoids the pitfalls of recursion like stack overflow for large inputs. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of * when the preceding character doesn't match, or off-by-one errors in indices. Always ensure base cases are correctly initialized and the DP transitions are applied in the right order.

examples.trace: Initialize dp table with n=2, m=2. dp[0][0]=True. Fill first row: dp[0][1]=False (p[0]='a' not '*'), dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True (p[1]='*', skip 'a'). Fill dp[1][1]: p[0]='a' matches s[0]='a', dp[0][0]=True → dp[1][1]=True. dp[1][2]: p[1]='*', either dp[1][0]=False (skip) or (match s[0]='a' with p[0]='a' and dp[0][2]=True) → True. dp[2][1]: p[0]='a' matches s[1]='a', dp[1][0]=False → False. dp[2][2]: p[1]='*', either dp[2][0]=False or (match s[1]='a' with p[0]='a' and dp[1][2]=True) → True. Final dp[2][2]=True → YES.

n=2, m=2. dp[0][0]=True. First row: dp[0][1]=False (p[0]='.'), dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True (p[1]='*', skip '.'). dp[1][1]: p[0]='.' matches any s[0]='a', dp[0][0]=True → True. dp[1][2]: p[1]='*', either dp[1][0]=False or (match s[0]='a' with p[0]='.' and dp[0][2]=True) → True. dp[2][1]: p[0]='.' matches s[1]='b', dp[1][0]=False → False. dp[2][2]: p[1]='*', either dp[2][0]=False or (match s[1]='b' with p[0]='.' and dp[1][2]=True) → True. Final dp[2][2]=True → YES.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Filling for * Handling
ladders.desc: Implement the core logic for filling DP table entries when pattern character is *.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the most critical part of the DP solution: handling the * character in the pattern. The intuition is that * allows zero or more repetitions of the preceding character. In DP terms, for dp[i][j] where p[j-1] is '*', we have two cases: 1) Zero occurrences: skip the pattern by looking at dp[i][j-2]. 2) One or more occurrences: match the current string character s[i-1] with the preceding pattern character p[j-2] (which could be a literal or '.'), and if it matches, look at dp[i-1][j] (since we consume one character from string but keep the pattern for potential more matches). The algorithm checks if p[j-1] == '*', then sets dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] OR (match(s[i-1], p[j-2]) AND dp[i-1][j]). The match function returns True if p[j-2] is '.' or equals s[i-1]. This sub-routine is called for each cell in the DP table after initializing base cases. Time complexity for this operation is O(1) per cell, but integrated into the overall O(n*m). Space is part of the DP table. It connects to L0 as the heart of the DP transitions. Common mistakes include forgetting to check j>=2 for dp[i][j-2], or incorrect match logic. Ensure indices are handled carefully, remembering that DP indices are 1-based relative to string and pattern.

examples.trace: Given dp table partially filled: dp[0][0]=True, dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=True, dp[1][1]=True. For i=1, j=2: p[j-1]=p[1]='*'. Case 1: dp[i][j-2]=dp[1][0]=False. Case 2: match(s[i-1]=s[0]='a', p[j-2]=p[0]='a') is True, and dp[i-1][j]=dp[0][2]=True → True. OR of cases → True. So dp[1][2]=True.

dp[0][0]=True, dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=True, dp[1][1]=False. For i=1, j=2: p[j-1]=p[1]='*'. Case 1 (zero occurrences): dp[1][0]=False. Case 2 (one+ occurrences): match(s[0]='b', p[0]='a') is False -> False. OR of both cases: False OR False = False. So dp[1][2]=False. (s='b' does not match p='a*' — zero 'a's reduces pattern to empty, which cannot match 'b'.)

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Definition and Transitions
ladders.desc: Define the DP state and general transition rules for regex matching.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves defining the DP state and the transition equations that solve the problem. The intuition is to break the problem into subproblems: whether prefixes of the string match prefixes of the pattern. Define dp[i][j] as a boolean: True if first i characters of s match first j characters of p. Base case: dp[0][0] = True (empty matches empty). For the first row (i=0, empty string), we need to handle patterns that can match empty via *: if p[j-1] is '*', then dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-2]. For i>0 and j>0, transitions depend on p[j-1]: if p[j-1] != '*', then dp[i][j] = match(s[i-1], p[j-1]) AND dp[i-1][j-1]. If p[j-1] == '*', then dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] OR (match(s[i-1], p[j-2]) AND dp[i-1][j]). The match function returns True if p char is '.' or equals s char. This formulation captures all cases: direct matching, . as wildcard, and * as repetition. Time and space are O(n*m) for building the table. This feeds into L1 by providing the framework for the * handling sub-routine. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases for empty string, or misindexing in transitions. Always use 1-based indices for dp relative to 0-based string/pattern.

examples.trace: n=1, m=1. dp[0][0]=True. First row: dp[0][1] not applicable since i>0. For i=1, j=1: p[0]='.' != '*'. match(s[0]='a', p[0]='.') is True. dp[i-1][j-1]=dp[0][0]=True. AND → True. So dp[1][1]=True.
n=0, m=2. dp[0][0]=True. First row for j=1: p[0]='a' != '*', so dp[0][1]=False. For j=2: p[1]='*', so dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True. Final dp[0][2]=True.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matching Function for Characters and '.'
ladders.desc: Implement a function to check if a string character matches a pattern character.
ladders.explanation: This building block is a helper function that determines if a single character from the string matches a single character from the pattern, accounting for the wildcard '.'. The intuition is simple: in regex, a literal character matches only itself, while '.' matches any single character. Formally, given char s_char and char p_char, return True if p_char == '.' or p_char == s_char, else False. This function is used in the DP transitions when checking if characters align before considering * or direct matches. For example, in dp[i][j] calculation, we often need match(s[i-1], p[j-1]) or match(s[i-1], p[j-2]). The algorithm is straightforward: if p_char == '.' or p_char == s_char: return True else False. Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). It connects to L2 as a fundamental component of the DP transitions. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle '.' or comparing indices incorrectly. Ensure the function is pure and doesn't modify any state.

examples.trace: p_char is 'a', which equals s_char 'a' → return True.
p_char is '.', which matches any character → return True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over a 2D Grid
ladders.desc: Perform nested iteration to fill a 2D array (DP table) in row-major order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through a 2D grid (like a DP table) using nested loops to fill each cell based on its indices. The intuition is that for dynamic programming, we often need to compute values in a specific order to ensure dependencies are satisfied—here, from smaller prefixes to larger ones. We use two loops: outer loop for i from 0 to n (string length), inner loop for j from 0 to m (pattern length). For bottom-up DP, we typically start from base cases and move forward. In this problem, we fill dp[i][j] for all i,j, often initializing dp[0][0] and then handling special cases for first row and column. The algorithm: initialize dp as (n+1) x (m+1) array with False. Set dp[0][0]=True. For j from 1 to m: if p[j-1]=='*', set dp[0][j]=dp[0][j-2]. Then for i from 1 to n: for j from 1 to m: apply DP transitions. Time complexity is O(n*m) for the iteration, space is O(n*m) for the table. This feeds into L3 by providing the structure to apply the matching function across the table. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, or filling in wrong order. Always ensure dimensions are correct and indices align with problem constraints.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[3][3] all False. Set dp[0][0]=True. First row loop j=1 to 2: for j=1, p[0] not '*' so dp[0][1]=False; for j=2, p[1]=='*' so dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True. Then nested loops: i=1, j=1: compute dp[1][1]; i=1, j=2: compute dp[1][2]; i=2, j=1: compute dp[2][1]; i=2, j=2: compute dp[2][2]. Each computation uses DP rules.
dp[2][2] all False. dp[0][0]=True. First row: j=1, p[0] not '*' so dp[0][1]=False. Nested: i=1, j=1: compute dp[1][1]. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding * as Zero or More Repetitions
ladders.desc: Learn the basic regex concept that * means zero or more of the preceding element.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for the problem: in regular expressions, the * character is a quantifier that matches zero or more occurrences of the preceding element. The intuition is that it allows flexibility—for example, pattern 'a*' can match '', 'a', 'aa', 'aaa', etc. The preceding element can be a literal character (like 'a') or a wildcard (like '.'). For instance, '.*' matches any sequence of characters, including empty. In terms of matching, when we see '*', we have two choices: 1) Skip it: match zero occurrences, so we ignore the preceding element and move on in the pattern. 2) Match one or more: consume one character from the string if it matches the preceding element, and then potentially match more. This concept is crucial for the DP approach because it defines the transitions when handling '*'. To internalize, think of examples: 'a*' matches 'b'? Yes, by matching zero 'a's, but then pattern reduces to empty, which doesn't match 'b'—so overall, 'a*' alone doesn't match 'b', but in combination like 'a*b', it might. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. This feeds into L4 by explaining why we need to iterate and make decisions in the DP table. Common mistakes include thinking * matches any character (it doesn't; it quantifies the preceding one) or misunderstanding zero case. Practice with simple strings and patterns to build intuition.

examples.trace: * allows zero 'a's, so pattern reduces to empty, which matches empty string.
* allows one or more 'a's: match first 'a', then pattern remains 'a*' for remaining 'aa', repeat.

approaches.name: Approach 4: Optimized DP with O(m) Space O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Refine the bottom-up DP by using only two rows or a rolling array to store necessary states, reducing space complexity. This is optimal for large inputs where memory is a constraint while maintaining the same time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Optimized DP Solution for Regular Expression Matching
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of regex matching with . and * using space-optimized bottom-up DP with O(m) space.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the classic regular expression matching problem where '.' matches any single character and '*' matches zero or more of the preceding element. We use a bottom-up dynamic programming approach with space optimization to achieve O(n*m) time and O(m) space complexity. The key insight is that we only need two rows of the DP table at any time - the current row being computed and the previous row. We maintain a DP array of size m+1 where dp[j] represents whether the first i characters of string s match the first j characters of pattern p. We iterate through s from 1 to n, and for each character, we compute the new DP row based on the previous row. The recurrence relation handles three cases: 1) When characters match or pattern has '.', we take the diagonal value. 2) When pattern has '*', we consider either zero occurrences (skip the pattern) or one or more occurrences (if preceding character matches). The space optimization comes from using only two arrays and swapping them after each iteration, reducing space from O(n*m) to O(m). This is particularly valuable for large inputs where memory constraints matter. The algorithm correctly handles edge cases like empty strings and patterns with multiple '*' operators.

examples.trace:
Initialize: prev = [True, False] (empty string matches empty pattern, but not 'a')
Iteration i=1 (s[0]='a'):
  curr[0] = False (non-empty string doesn't match empty pattern)
  For j=1 (p[0]='a'):
    Match = 'a' == 'a' → True
    Since no '*', curr[1] = prev[0] = True
  curr becomes [False, True]
  prev = curr
Iteration i=2 (s[1]='a'):
  curr[0] = False
  For j=1 (p[0]='a'):
    Match = 'a' == 'a' → True
    curr[1] = prev[0] = False
  curr becomes [False, False]
  prev = curr
After loop: prev[1] = False, but we need to check pattern with '*'
Actually, let me correct: p='a*' means we need to handle '*' at position 1
Proper trace:
Initialize prev = [True, False, False] (for pattern positions: 0:'', 1:'a', 2:'a*')
For i=1 (s[0]='a'):
  curr[0] = False
  For j=1: 'a' matches 'a', curr[1] = prev[0] = True
  For j=2: p[1]='*', check zero occurrence: curr[2] = curr[0] = False
    Check one+ occurrence: 'a' matches preceding 'a', so curr[2] = curr[2] OR prev[2] = False OR False = False
  Actually wait, prev[2] was False
  Set curr = [False, True, False], prev = curr
For i=2 (s[1]='a'):
  curr[0] = False
  For j=1: 'a' matches 'a', curr[1] = prev[0] = False
  For j=2: p[1]='*', zero: curr[2] = curr[0] = False
    one+: 'a' matches 'a', curr[2] = curr[2] OR prev[2] = False OR False = False
  curr = [False, False, False]
  prev = curr
Final check: We need to look at prev[m] where m=2, which is False
Wait, I made an error. Let me trace properly:
Actually for pattern 'a*', the DP should work like this:
Base: dp[0][0] = True, dp[0][1] = False, dp[0][2] = True (empty string matches 'a*' with zero 'a's)
For i=1: s[0]='a'
  dp[1][0] = False
  dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] = True (match 'a' with 'a')
  dp[1][2] = dp[1][0] OR (match and dp[0][2]) = False OR (True and True) = True
For i=2: s[1]='a'
  dp[2][0] = False
  dp[2][1] = dp[1][0] = False
  dp[2][2] = dp[2][0] OR (match and dp[1][2]) = False OR (True and True) = True
Final: dp[2][2] = True → YES

Pattern: .* means zero or more of any character
Initialize prev = [True, False, False] (positions: 0:'', 1:'.', 2:'.*')
Actually prev[2] should be True for empty string matching '.*'
Let me correct: For pattern with '*', we need to initialize properly
Better initialization:
prev = [True] + [False]*2 = [True, False, False]
But for j=2 where p[1]='*', we set prev[2] = prev[0] = True (zero occurrences)
So prev = [True, False, True]
Now iterate:
i=1 (s[0]='a'):
  curr[0] = False
  For j=1 (p[0]='.'): match = True, curr[1] = prev[0] = True
  For j=2 (p[1]='*'):
    zero = curr[0] = False
    one+ = match and prev[2] where match = 'a' matches '.' = True
    So curr[2] = zero OR (True and True) = True
  curr = [False, True, True], prev = curr
i=2 (s[1]='b'):
  curr[0] = False
  For j=1: match = '.' matches 'b' = True, curr[1] = prev[0] = False
  For j=2: zero = curr[0] = False, one+ = match and prev[2] = True and True = True
    curr[2] = False OR True = True
  curr = [False, False, True], prev = curr
Final: prev[2] = True → YES

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Space-Optimized DP Row Computation
ladders.desc: Compute a single DP row for regex matching given previous row and current string character.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes one row of the DP table in the optimized solution. Given the previous DP row (representing matches up to i-1 characters of string s), the current character s[i-1], and the pattern p, it computes the new DP row for matches up to i characters. The algorithm processes each position j in the pattern from 1 to m. For each position, it handles three cases: 1) If the current pattern character is not '*', it checks if s[i-1] matches p[j-1] (either equal or p[j-1] is '.'). If they match, the value comes from the diagonal (previous row, previous column). 2) If p[j-1] is '*', it considers two possibilities: zero occurrences of the preceding character (value from two columns left in current row) or one or more occurrences (if s[i-1] matches the character before '*', take value from previous row at current column). The key optimization is that we only need the previous row and we're building the current row left-to-right, so we can use a single array and update it in place with careful ordering. However, to avoid overwriting values needed later, we typically use two arrays and swap them. This sub-routine is the core computational step that gets repeated for each character in s, reducing the space from O(n*m) to O(m). The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(m) per string character.

examples.input: prev=[True, False, True], s_char='a', p='a*', i=1
examples.trace:
Initialize curr = [False]*3
curr[0] = False (non-empty string doesn't match empty pattern)
For j=1 (p[0]='a'):
  s_char='a' matches p[0]='a' → True
  curr[1] = prev[0] = True
For j=2 (p[1]='*'):
  zero_occurrence = curr[0] = False
  one_plus_occurrence = (s_char matches char before '*') and prev[2]
  'a' matches 'a' → True
  prev[2] = True
  So one_plus = True and True = True
  curr[2] = zero_occurrence OR one_plus_occurrence = False OR True = True
Final curr = [False, True, True]

examples.input: prev=[False, True, True], s_char='b', p='.*', i=2
examples.trace:
Initialize curr = [False]*3
curr[0] = False
For j=1 (p[0]='.'):
  s_char='b' matches '.' → True
  curr[1] = prev[0] = False
For j=2 (p[1]='*'):
  zero_occurrence = curr[0] = False
  one_plus_occurrence = (s_char matches '.') and prev[2]
  'b' matches '.' → True
  prev[2] = True
  So one_plus = True and True = True
  curr[2] = False OR True = True
Final curr = [False, False, True]

testCases.input: prev=[True, False, False], s_char='a', p='ab', i=1
prev=[True, False, True], s_char='b', p='a*', i=1
prev=[True, False, True], s_char='c', p='.*', i=1
prev=[False, True, False], s_char='a', p='a*a', i=2
prev=[True, False, False, True], s_char='b', p='a*b', i=2
prev=[False, False, True, False], s_char='a', p='a*a', i=2
prev=[False, True, False], s_char='', p='a*', i=0
prev=[True, False, False], s_char='a', p='a.*', i=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Regex with . and *
ladders.desc: The mathematical recurrence that defines whether s[0:i] matches p[0:j].
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental recurrence relation that drives the DP solution. Let dp[i][j] represent whether the first i characters of string s match the first j characters of pattern p. The recurrence has three main cases based on the pattern character p[j-1]: 1) If p[j-1] is not '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] if s[i-1] matches p[j-1] (either equal or p[j-1] is '.'), otherwise false. 2) If p[j-1] is '*': We consider the character before '*', which is p[j-2]. Now we have two possibilities: a) Zero occurrences of p[j-2]: dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2]. b) One or more occurrences: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] if s[i-1] matches p[j-2]. The overall value is OR of these two possibilities. The base cases are: dp[0][0] = true (empty matches empty), dp[i][0] = false for i>0 (non-empty string doesn't match empty pattern), and dp[0][j] needs special handling for patterns ending with '*'. For dp[0][j] where p[j-1] is '*', we have dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-2] (zero occurrences). This recurrence captures all possibilities of matching with '.' and '*'. The '.' acts as a wildcard that matches any single character. The '*' allows zero or more repetitions of the preceding character, which requires considering both skipping the character-star pair or using it to match current string character. This recurrence is optimal because it considers all possible ways the pattern could match the string without exponential backtracking.

examples.input: i=2, j=2, s='aa', p='a*', dp[1][1]=True, dp[2][0]=False, dp[1][2]=True
examples.trace:
p[j-1] = p[1] = '*'
Character before '*' = p[0] = 'a'
Case 1 (zero occurrences): dp[2][2] = dp[2][0] = False
Case 2 (one+ occurrences): Check if s[i-1] = s[1] = 'a' matches p[j-2] = 'a' → True
  So dp[2][2] = dp[1][2] = True
Combine with OR: False OR True = True

p[j-1] = p[0] = '.' (not '*')
Check if s[i-1] = 'a' matches p[j-1] = '.' → '.' matches any character, so True
dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] = True

testCases.input: i=0, j=0, s='', p='', dp values n/a
i=1, j=0, s='a', p='', dp values n/a
i=2, j=3, s='aa', p='a*a', dp[2][1]=False, dp[1][3]=True
i=3, j=3, s='aaa', p='a*a', dp[3][1]=False, dp[2][3]=True
i=2, j=4, s='xb', p='x.*b', dp[2][2]=True, dp[1][4]=False

ladders.title: Building Block — Pattern Preprocessing for * Handling
ladders.desc: Initialize DP base cases accounting for * patterns matching empty string.
ladders.explanation: Before starting the main DP computation, we need to properly initialize the first row (dp[0][j]) which represents whether an empty string matches the first j characters of the pattern. This is crucial because patterns with '*' can match zero occurrences, allowing them to match empty strings. The initialization process scans the pattern from left to right. We start with dp[0][0] = True (empty string matches empty pattern). For each position j from 1 to m: if p[j-1] is '*', then dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-2] (we can skip the character-star pair). Otherwise, dp[0][j] = False (non-star pattern character cannot match empty string). This preprocessing ensures our DP table starts with correct values for the empty string case. For example, pattern 'a*b*c*' should match empty string because each starred character can appear zero times. The initialization would set dp[0][2] = True (for 'a*'), dp[0][4] = True (for 'a*b*'), and dp[0][6] = True (for 'a*b*c*'). This step is essential because the main recurrence relation for '*' references dp[i][j-2], which relies on these base values being correct. Without proper initialization, the algorithm would fail on patterns that should match empty strings. The time complexity is O(m) for this preprocessing.

examples.trace:
Initialize dp[0] array of size 3 (m+1 where m=2)
dp[0][0] = True
j=1: p[0]='a' (not '*') → dp[0][1] = False
j=2: p[1]='*' → dp[0][2] = dp[0][0] = True
Result: [True, False, True]

m=4, array size 5
dp[0][0] = True
j=1: p[0]='a' → False
j=2: p[1]='*' → dp[0][2] = dp[0][0] = True
j=3: p[2]='b' → False
j=4: p[3]='*' → dp[0][4] = dp[0][2] = True
Result: [True, False, True, False, True]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Matching with Wildcard
ladders.desc: Check if a string character matches a pattern character considering '.' wildcard.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether a single character from the string matches a single character from the pattern, accounting for the '.' wildcard character. The rule is simple: if the pattern character is '.', it matches any string character. Otherwise, it only matches if the string character equals the pattern character exactly. This operation is used throughout the DP algorithm whenever we need to compare s[i-1] with p[j-1]. For example, 'a' matches 'a' (true), 'a' matches '.' (true), but 'a' matches 'b' (false). Note that this operation does NOT handle '*' - that's a separate operation. The '*' is handled at a higher level because it involves matching zero or more characters, not just one. This basic matching is the building block for more complex pattern matching. In the DP recurrence, this check appears in two places: 1) When the pattern character is not '*', we use this to determine if we can take the diagonal value. 2) When the pattern character is '*', we use this to check if the current string character matches the character before the '*'. The operation has O(1) time complexity and is purely a character comparison.

examples.trace:
Pattern character is '.', which matches any single character.
'c' is a character, so match = True.

Pattern character is 'y', not '.'.
Compare 'x' == 'y' → False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding * as Zero-or-More Operator
ladders.desc: Learn how * in regular expressions allows zero or more repetitions of preceding element.
ladders.explanation: In regular expressions, the '*' operator means 'zero or more occurrences of the preceding element'. This is different from multiplication or other uses of asterisk. The preceding element can be a single character (like 'a*') or a wildcard (like '.*'). There are two key cases to understand: 1) Zero occurrences: The pattern 'a*' can match an empty string by taking zero 'a's. 2) One or more occurrences: 'a*' can match 'a', 'aa', 'aaa', etc. The '*' is always applied to the character immediately before it. For example, in pattern 'a*b', the '*' applies only to 'a', not to 'b'. So 'a*b' can match 'b' (zero 'a's), 'ab' (one 'a'), 'aab' (two 'a's), etc. When combined with '.', '.*' means zero or more of any character, making it very powerful. This operator requires non-greedy thinking: we must consider both possibilities (zero times or one+ times) at each matching decision. In algorithms, this typically leads to either backtracking or dynamic programming to explore these possibilities. Understanding this operator is foundational because it's what makes regex matching challenging - without '*', we could just compare character by character.

examples.trace:
'a*' means zero or more 'a's.
Zero 'a's means empty string.
Empty string has zero 'a's, so it matches.

'a*' means zero or more 'a's.
'aaa' has three 'a's.
Three is more than zero, so it matches.
We use the '*' to match all three 'a's as multiple occurrences of 'a'.
